hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: The property has value as an early-twentieth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of early-twentieth-century architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, the property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. The property retains most of its original building features including brick cladding, flat headed openings, projecting two-storey bay, second storey oriel window and hipped attic dormer. Contextually, the property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. The property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

context.street_character: One-way | Residential | Active laneway housing development

facade_detail.heritage_summary: Contextually, the property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. The property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

facade_description: 85 Wales Ave presents a house-form, detached facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 3 storeys and is capped by a gable, consistent with its house-form, detached typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2, 1 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double-hung sash windows, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include bay window, decorative brick, original windows. HCD note: The property has value as an early-twentieth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of early-twentieth-century architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, the property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. The property retains most of its original building features including brick cladding, flat headed openings, projecting two-storey bay, second storey oriel window and hipped attic dormer. Contextually, the property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. The property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

The property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.
